nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline and intermediate zones of Mbenji Island, Lake Malawi; found in the sediment-free boulder habitat typical of mbuna cichlids, at moderate depths

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks forming caves and crevices
open sandy substrate between rock structures
minimal or no plants (uprooted by digging)
dense rockwork to break line of sight and distribute aggression

diet.notes: Primarily herbivorous in the wild, grazing on aufwuchs (algae and microorganisms encrusting rocks). In aquaria, offer spirulina-based flakes and pellets as the staple, supplemented with vegetable matter (blanched zucchini, spinach), high-quality cichlid pellets, and occasional frozen foods (mysis, brine shrimp). Avoid high-protein meaty foods fed continuously — Malawi bloat risk is elevated with excessive animal protein for mbuna. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: other robust Lake Malawi mbuna cichlids of similar size (e.g., Pseudotropheus, Labeotropheus, Melanochromis)
kept in species colonies or with equally aggressive mbuna
large, fast-moving non-cichlid dither fish if tank is very large
avoidWith: peaceful or semi-aggressive fish of any kind
smaller or less aggressive cichlid species
invertebrates (shrimp, snails will be harassed or eaten)
Haplochromine cichlids (Haps/Peacocks) unless tank is very large — mbuna will relentlessly harass
conspecific males kept together in small tanks (extreme male–male aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Maylandia lombardoi (listed as Metriaclima lombardoi on FishBase); shows adult male in yellow coloration. Verify availability before use.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult male Maylandia lombardoi in yellow phase; good quality reference photo.

summary: Lake Malawi endemic from Mbenji Island; one of the most aggressive mbuna species. Remarkable for fully reversed sexual dichromatism — males are bright yellow/orange, females are powder blue with dark vertical bars, opposite to virtually all other mbuna. Maternal mouthbrooder. Expert-level aggression management required.
careNotes:
The Lombardoi Mbuna is widely regarded as one of the most belligerent mbuna species in the hobby and demands advanced-level husbandry focused almost entirely on managing extreme aggression. A minimum 300 L tank (150 cm footprint) is recommended; larger is strongly preferred. The tank must be heavily rockscaped with numerous caves, overhangs, and sight-line breaks — aggression will concentrate on any individual that cannot escape or hide. The standard mbuna overstocking strategy applies: keeping a larger colony (8–12+ individuals) dilutes aggression across multiple targets and reduces the risk of a single subordinate fish being hounded to death.

This species exhibits fully reversed sexual dichromatism relative to typical mbuna norms: females are powder blue with dark vertical barring (the pattern most mbuna males display), while males develop bright yellow to orange-yellow coloration with faint barring. Juveniles of both sexes begin blue; males transition to yellow as they mature — sellers sometimes mislabel juveniles or keep them labeled as female. Inform purchasers of the sex-change coloration.

The genus assignment is contested. Stauffer (2004) placed the species in Maylandia; Konings and Stauffer (2018) maintained Metriaclima as the valid genus, arguing Maylandia is a junior synonym. Both names remain in active use across the scientific and hobbyist literature; FishBase and SeriouslyFish currently list the species under Metriaclima lombardoi, while a portion of the hobby uses Maylandia. Either name is acceptable; the debate is ongoing.

Maintain hard, alkaline water matching Lake Malawi conditions (pH 7.8–8.5, gH 10–20, kH 8–16, temperature 24–28°C). Weekly water changes of 25–30% help export nitrates that accumulate with high stocking densities. Malawi bloat (internal parasitic/bacterial condition triggered by dietary stress and poor water quality) is a real risk — feed a primarily herbivorous diet and avoid overfeeding meaty proteins. Lake Malawi salt additives or crushed coral substrate help maintain carbonate hardness and buffer pH.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning occurs on flat rocks or open substrate following a typical cichlid "egg-spot" courtship display, during which the female pecks at the egg-spot markings on the male's anal fin and takes up sperm-packets. The female collects and orally incubates 15–30 eggs for approximately 21–28 days at 26°C, during which she does not feed. Fry are released as fully formed, free-swimming juveniles 8–12 mm long and begin feeding immediately on crushed flake or finely ground cichlid pellet. The female should be given a low-aggression tank or separated to a holding tank during incubation to prevent stress-induced spitting of the brood. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate — pairs spawn readily in established mbuna colonies, but removing the female during holding and raising fry safely requires dedicated effort. Juveniles are blue (female-like) at birth; male coloration transition to yellow begins at ~3–4 months.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Metriaclima lombardoi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Mbenji Island, Lake Malawi), adult size (~14 cm), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.8, temperature 23–26°C), diet (aufwuchs grazer/omnivore), aggression level, reversed sexual dichromatism, mouthbrooder details, and minimum tank size guidance. Species listed under Metriaclima lombardoi per FishBase/SeriouslyFish current nomenclature; Maylandia lombardoi per Stauffer 2004 — both names used in hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase — Metriaclima lombardoi
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size (males to 14 cm TL), native range (Mbenji Island endemic), depth range, and environmental parameters. FishBase currently lists the species as Metriaclima lombardoi, noting Maylandia lombardoi as a synonym (Stauffer 2004); Konings & Stauffer (2018) retain Metriaclima. Used Maylandia in id/scientificName per task specification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care
additional.notes: General Lake Malawi mbuna husbandry guidance; confirmed herbivore-leaning diet requirements, Malawi bloat risk, tank size recommendations (55+ gallons for mbuna), rockscape strategy, and water parameter targets for the Lake Malawi biotope.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lake Malawi cichlids
additional.notes: Confirmed reversed sexual dichromatism (females blue, males yellow/orange) and the species' reputation as one of the most aggressive mbuna; supported the overstocking dilution strategy for aggression management and rocky habitat requirements.

additional.name: Konings, A. & Stauffer, J.R. (2018) — Nomenclature review of Metriaclima/Maylandia
additional.notes: Documents the ongoing genus debate: Stauffer (2004) erected Maylandia; Konings & Stauffer (2018) argued Metriaclima has priority, making Maylandia a junior synonym. Both names remain in wide use. This entry uses Maylandia lombardoi per task specification but careNotes acknowledge the dispute.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 15–30 eggs for ~21–28 days orally. Spawning is readily triggered in stable mbuna colonies, but successful fry-raising requires isolating the holding female to prevent brood loss from stress. Juveniles begin blue; males yellow-shift at ~3–4 months.
